claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you a few questions. Remember, Child Benefit is assessed separately for each child.

First question: **Do you have a child or children you're responsible for? If yes, how old is the child (or how old are the children)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children. Alex is 14 and Blake is 19.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. You have two children - Alex (14) and Blake (19). I'll need to assess eligibility for each of them separately.

Let me start with some questions:

**Does Alex (your 14-year-old) work full-time, meaning 24 hours or more per week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, thank you. Now for Alex:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now let me ask about Blake (your 19-year-old). Since Blake is 19, there are additional requirements for eligibility:

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training?** This would include things like:
- Full-time non-advanced education (A-levels, Scottish Highers, etc.)
- Approved training courses
- Courses up to and including level 3 (or equivalent)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is in approved education - studying for A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Blake is studying A-levels, which is approved education. Now I need to ask a few more questions about Blake:

**Does Blake work 24 hours or more per week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work that much.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now:

**Is Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now I need to ask about your circumstances. 

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good. Now I need to ask about your responsibility for your children:

**Do both Alex and Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Blake lives with you, but Alex doesn't.

For Alex (who doesn't live with you): **Do you contribute to Alex's upkeep?** This could include paying for things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or other support - at least equivalent to the Child Benefit amount.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't contribute financially to Alex's upkeep at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Since Alex doesn't live with you and you don't contribute to their upkeep, you would not meet the responsibility requirement for Alex.

Now, moving on to Blake who does live with you - that means you are responsible for Blake.

**Is there anyone else who is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or who has a stronger claim than you?** For example, another parent or guardian who also lives with Blake or who might have priority?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually someone else who lives with Blake has priority and is already claiming for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see. Let me clarify: **Are you and the other person in agreement that they should be the one claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or is this a situation where you both want to claim and there's a disagreement?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We're in agreement - they should be the one claiming for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since you're in agreement that the other person should claim for Blake, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake - only one person can claim for each child, and you've agreed that the other person has priority.

Let me now summarize the situation for both of your children:

**For Alex (14 years old):**
- Alex doesn't live with you
- You don't contribute financially to Alex's upkeep
- Therefore, you don't meet the "responsibility" requirement for Alex

**For Blake (19 years old):**
- Although Blake lives with you and is in approved education (A-levels)
- Someone else is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you're in agreement that they should be the claimant
- Therefore, you cannot claim for Blake as only one person can claim per child

Based on this assessment, **you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake** due to the responsibility requirements for Alex and the existing agreed claim for Blake.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex comes to live with you, or if you start contributing to their upkeep, or if the arrangement for Blake changes - you may want to reassess your eligibility at that time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit eligibility?
